t: Clinical interview — onset, frequency, daytime symptoms, family history.
Urinalysis and culture — rule out UTI.
Voiding diary 3 days — for daytime symptoms
If structural anomaly suspected - urinary USG; uroflowmetry in non-monosymptomatic case.
Conservative recommendations — reducing fluid intake before bedtime, adequate water intake during the day, regular urination, resolving constipation.
Positive reinforcement (star chart) — for dry nights.
— pad-and-bell; ≥ 6 years old, if motivated; long-term good outcome; 65–75% effective. Evidence: Caldwell P.H. Cochrane 2020.
— synthetic ADH analog; rapid effect, but high relapse upon discontinuation. ≥ 6–7 years.
Anticholinergic (oxybutynin) — in the context of non-monosymptomatic overactive bladder.
imipramine (TCA) — cardiac monitoring; use cautiously, overdose toxicity.
— PEG (polyethylene glycol) first-line; in non-monosymptomatic or comorbid encopresis context.
ICCS 2020 — alarm and desmopressin first-line; "wait and see" until age 5, then active intervention.
Bed alarm or clothing alarm (humidity sensor); ≥ 4 weeks continuous use; typical use 12–16 weeks. Cochrane meta-analysis — most evidence-based long-term method.
— Oral 0.2–0.4 mg or sublingual; 1 hour before bedtime; limiting fluids before bedtime is important (risk of hyponatremia).
3-day; frequency, volume, bladder capacity, daytime symptoms, defecation frequency.
— Against background of over-active bladder; if daytime symptoms are present.
Spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 15% per year; with intervention, remission occurs in most patients.
Myth 1: "In nocturnal enuresis, electrophoresis to the groin and buttock area stimulates the urinary center"
Soviet-era physiotherapy practice; presented as "safe" by family doctors.

If daytime urine output is normal and the problem did not spontaneously arise after any illness, the problem is NOT at the kidney/bladder/spinal cord level. Pathogenesis is ADH dysregulation, arousal threshold and bladder capacity — electrophoresis does not affect these.
ICCS 2020, NICE CG111 — no international guideline includes electrophoresis. Risk: skin irritation, psychosexual burden.
Myth 2: "It will just pass on its own, no intervention needed"
Spontaneous remission ~15% per year, but untreated persistence creates socio-emotional burden; ICCS 2020 recommends active intervention from age 5+.
Myth 3: "Punishment or insult corrects a child"
The punishment is external; self-esteem reduction, depression risk; positive reinforcement is superior.
Myth 4: "Pre-bedtime water restriction alone is sufficient"
Adjunct measure; ineffective alone in most patients; requires specific intervention.
imipramine in refractory cases; cardiac side effects and overdose risk; alarm and desmopressin superior.
Cochrane reviews — the effect of these complementary interventions has not been proven.
NICE CG111. Nocturnal enuresis in children and young people under 19. 2010.
Nevéus T. et al. Management and treatment of nocturnal enuresis — an updated standardization document from the International Children's Continence Society. J Pediatr Urol 2020;16(1):10–19.
Caldwell P.H., Codarini M., Stewart F. et al. Alarm interventions for nocturnal enuresis in children. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2020;(5):CD002911.
